audit_metadata.audit_type: Security Audit for RCE/Command Injection Vulnerabilities
audit_metadata.incident_context: Cryptocurrency miner malware discovered running as user 999 in PostgreSQL container

command_injection.vulnerability_description: The glob CLI has a command injection vulnerability via the -c/--cmd parameter when shell:true is used. This allows arbitrary shell command execution.
command_injection.attack_vector: If the API internally uses glob with shell commands based on user input, attackers could inject arbitrary shell commands

remediation.immediate: Update @nestjs/cli to >=10.5.0 which includes patched glob

command_injection.impact: Remote Code Execution with Node.js process privileges

file_operations.dependency_chain: apps/api > @coffeeshop/database > bcrypt@5.1.1 > @mapbox/node-pre-gyp@1.0.11 > tar@6.2.1 | apps/api > bcrypt@5.1.1 > @mapbox/node-pre-gyp@1.0.11 > tar@6.2.1

vulnerabilities.description: Vulnerable to Arbitrary File Creation/Overwrite via Hardlink Path Traversal
Vulnerable to Arbitrary File Overwrite and Symlink Poisoning via Insufficient Path Sanitization
Target Escape Through Symlink Chain in tar Extraction

file_operations.attack_vector: If bcrypt (used for password hashing) is exploited during installation or binary compilation, attackers could extract tar archives with crafted symlinks/hardlinks to write arbitrary files to filesystem
file_operations.impact: Arbitrary file write, potential code execution if written to executable locations, container filesystem compromise

deserialization.vulnerability_description: Next.js HTTP request deserialization can lead to DoS when using insecure React Server Components with untrusted payload deserialization
deserialization.attack_vector: Malicious HTTP requests with crafted payloads during deserialization could crash the admin dashboard or expose memory

remediation.immediate: This is in admin-dashboard, not active API but affects admin connectivity

deserialization.impact: Denial of Service, potential information disclosure

high_severity_vulnerabilities.vulnerability_description: Lodash _.unset and _.omit functions are vulnerable to prototype pollution via array path bypass. An attacker with control over the path parameter could pollute Object prototypes.
high_severity_vulnerabilities.attack_vector: If API processes user input through lodash utility functions in @nestjs/cli tools, prototype pollution could occur
high_severity_vulnerabilities.impact: Prototype pollution could affect all objects in Node process, potential code execution

high_severity_vulnerabilities.vulnerability_description: Multiple ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service) vulnerabilities in brace-expansion and zero-step sequence causing process hang and memory exhaustion
high_severity_vulnerabilities.attack_vector: Crafted glob patterns with specific brace expansion syntax could cause CPU exhaustion and DoS

remediation.immediate: Update packages that depend on brace-expansion

high_severity_vulnerabilities.impact: Denial of Service during application startup or API usage

high_severity_vulnerabilities.vulnerability_description: Method Injection in POSIX Character Classes causes incorrect glob pattern matching. An attacker could craft patterns that bypass security checks or match unintended files.
high_severity_vulnerabilities.attack_vector: If file watching or pattern matching is done on user-controlled paths, malicious patterns could match sensitive files
high_severity_vulnerabilities.impact: Incorrect glob matching, potential file traversal or unintended file operations

code_level_vulnerabilities.vulnerability_type: SQL Injection via Prisma (Low risk due to parameterization)
code_level_vulnerabilities.description: While using Prisma ORM which provides parameterized queries, the endpoint accepts raw pin/storeId from query parameters without strict validation. trim() is not sufficient.
code_level_vulnerabilities.potential_exploit: Although Prisma prevents SQL injection, lack of input validation could allow bypass attacks or type confusion

remediation.step3: Add rate limiting on PIN verification endpoint to prevent brute force

code_level_vulnerabilities.code_snippet:
const message = `
☕ *Nouvelle Commande B2B - ${storeName}* ☕
Bonjour ${supplierName},

Vous avez une nouvelle suggestion de commande (Réf: #${orderId.slice(-6)}).
Voici les articles critiques à livrer :

${itemsDescription}

👉 Répondez *CONFIRMER* à ce message pour valider la livraison.
`;
code_level_vulnerabilities.description: While this is JavaScript template literals (not SSTI), the message content includes unsanitized user inputs (supplierName, storeName, itemsDescription) that are sent to WhatsApp. If WhatsApp API processes special formatting, this could be exploited.
code_level_vulnerabilities.potential_exploit: Attackers could inject WhatsApp formatting codes or special characters through supplier/store names to manipulate message display or inject content

remediation.step1: Sanitize all user inputs before inserting into messages
remediation.step2: Use a messaging template library with escaping

docker_and_deployment_vulnerabilities.vulnerability_type: Running as Root in Multi-stage Build
docker_and_deployment_vulnerabilities.description: The runner stage sets WORKDIR /app but doesn't specify a non-root user. Container runs as root by default.
docker_and_deployment_vulnerabilities.code_snippet:
FROM base AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV production
COPY --from=builder /app /app
EXPOSE 3000 3001

CMD ["pnpm", "start"]
docker_and_deployment_vulnerabilities.potential_exploit: If container is compromised, attacker has root-level access. This likely enabled the cryptocurrency miner to: 1) Download binaries to /tmp/, 2) Execute with postgres user (999) permissions via PostgreSQL database commands, 3) Auto-restart via container orchestration
docker_and_deployment_vulnerabilities.attack_flow:
1. Attacker exploits API vulnerability → RCE in Node container (user: node)
2. Attacker escalates to root (if possible) or uses existing database connection
3. Places malware binaries in /tmp with postgres owner (user 999)
4. Malware executes with container restart persistence mechanism

remediation.step1: Create non-root user in runner stage
remediation.example_code:
FROM base AS runner
RUN addgroup -g 1000 -S nodejs
RUN adduser -S nodejs -u 1000
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV production
COPY --from=builder --chown=nodejs:nodejs /app /app
USER nodejs
EXPOSE 3000 3001
CMD ["pnpm", "start"]

docker_and_deployment_vulnerabilities.description: PostgreSQL service uses weak default credentials: postgres:postgres
docker_and_deployment_vulnerabilities.potential_exploit: Default credentials allow attackers who gain network access to the database to execute queries. From database, attackers can: 1) Read sensitive data, 2) Modify tables, 3) Potentially execute system commands if PostgreSQL extensions are available

docker_and_deployment_vulnerabilities.description: PostgreSQL service exposes port 5433 on host machine, making database accessible from outside container network
docker_and_deployment_vulnerabilities.potential_exploit: If VPS firewall isn't properly configured, database is accessible from internet. Brute force attacks against database credentials possible. The cryptocurrency miner may have: 1) Connected to exposed database port, 2) Executed admin commands, 3) Used database to deploy malware or schedule jobs

remediation.step1: Remove port exposure from docker-compose.yml or restrict in production
remediation.step2: Only expose if necessary for development
remediation.step3: Add firewall rules to restrict access
remediation.example_code:
# For production: don't expose the port
# For development only:
ports:
  - '127.0.0.1:5433:5432'  # Only accessible from localhost

environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.problem: Default JWT secret 'supers3cr3tc0ffeesh0p' is hardcoded. If .env file is missing or on production VPS, this weak secret is used.
environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.impact: Attackers can forge valid JWT tokens, impersonate any user, bypass authentication
environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.remediation:
1) Remove default value and require .env setting
2) Use strong random 32+ character secret
3) Rotate secret regularly

environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.issue: Environment Variables Exposed via Process Inspection
environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.problem: DATABASE_URL with plaintext credentials is passed as environment variable to container
environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.impact:
If Node.js process is compromised, $: ps aux | grep NODE shows DATABASE_URL in environment
Malware running as postgres user (999) could read process environment of Node process
environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.remediation:
1) Use Docker secrets instead of environment variables for sensitive data
2) Use Node.js dotenv with .env.local not exposed in docker-compose
3) Implement credential rotation

environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.problem: Even though only ['error'] is logged in production, large errors could be logged that contain sensitive data
environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.impact: Application errors logged could contain database queries or user data
environmental_and_configuration_vulnerab.remediation:
1) Implement proper error handling with sanitization
2) Use structured logging with log levels
3) Forward logs to secure logging service (not stdout)

likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.name: SQL Injection or Command Injection via API
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.description: Although Prisma prevents SQL injection, if there are any raw SQL queries or dynamic query building, attackers could execute shell commands via PostgreSQL extensions.
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.how_it_worked:
1. Attacker sends malicious request to API endpoint
2. API constructs query with user input
3. Query executes system command via PostgreSQL (if enabled)
4. Command downloads/executes /tmp/mysql binary
5. Binary runs as postgres user (999) inside container
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.evidence_from_incident: Malware was found running as user 999 (postgres) in coffeeshop_postgres container

likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.name: Supply Chain Attack via Dependencies
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.description: Multiple high/critical CVEs in dependencies could have been exploited during installation or runtime
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.how_it_worked:
1. Attacker publishes malicious npm package version
2. pnpm install runs during Docker build
3. Malicious package extracts files to /tmp/ with execute permissions
4. Application startup triggers malware execution
5. Persistence mechanism (cron/watchdog) keeps malware running
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.evidence_from_incident: Malware respawned automatically within 2 seconds after kill - indicates persistence mechanism

likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.name: Environment Variable Injection or File Upload
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.description: If there are any file upload endpoints or environment variable processing, attackers could inject malicious executables
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.how_it_worked:
1. Attacker uploads file or injects env var
2. Application processes it without proper validation
3. File placed in /tmp/ with execute permissions
4. Application or external process (postgres via extension) executes file
5. Malware achieves RCE as postgres user
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.note: No explicit file upload endpoints found in code review, but /tmp is writeable by all users

likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.name: PostgreSQL Extension/Function Exploitation
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.description: PostgreSQL can execute system commands via functions like pg_exec or creating custom extensions
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.how_it_worked:
1. Attacker gains database access (via API or exposed port 5433)
2. Creates malicious PostgreSQL function: CREATE FUNCTION ... AS 'exec('
3. Function downloads malware to /tmp/
4. Function executes malware with postgres user privileges
5. Malware becomes cryptocurrency miner consuming CPU
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.evidence_from_incident: Malware running as postgres user (999) suggests database-level execution

likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.name: Weak/Default Credentials on Exposed Database
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.description: PostgreSQL port 5433 exposed on host with default credentials postgres:postgres
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.how_it_worked:
1. Attacker scans VPS ports and finds 5433 open
2. Connects to PostgreSQL with default credentials
3. Executes CREATE EXTENSION pg_execute_sql or similar
4. Downloads/executes malware binary
5. Achieves RCE with postgres user privileges
likely_attack_vectors_that_enabled_malwa.evidence_from_incident: Docker logs and configuration show port 5433:5432 exposed

IMMEDIATE_CRITICAL.impact: Prevents command injection attacks via glob patterns

IMMEDIATE_CRITICAL.action: Update bcrypt and patch tar vulnerabilities (CV-002)

HIGH_PRIORITY.command: Change ports section or remove it, add firewall rules
HIGH_PRIORITY.impact: Prevents direct database access from internet

HIGH_PRIORITY.command: Implement DTO validation on all endpoints

MEDIUM_PRIORITY.action: Implement rate limiting on authentication endpoints
MEDIUM_PRIORITY.impact: Prevents brute force attacks on PIN/password endpoints

MEDIUM_PRIORITY.command: Add Winston or Pino logger with log sanitization
Add sanitization function for supplier/store names

forensic_analysis_of_incident.incident_summary: Cryptocurrency miner executed with postgres user (999) privileges inside PostgreSQL container

timeline.t_minus_unknown: Attacker exploits vulnerability to gain initial access
timeline.t_minus_10_min: Malware process /tmp/mysql spawned and starts consuming CPU
timeline.t_0_min: Administrator detects 392% CPU usage via monitoring
timeline.t_plus_2_sec: Process respawns as PID 67839 - indicates persistence mechanism
timeline.t_plus_5_min:
Files cleaned from /tmp directory:
- /tmp/mysql (2.0MB) - main malware binary
- /tmp/init (2.7MB) - initialization/dropper script
timeline.t_plus_10_min: Container restarted to clear process table

forensic_findings.process_memory: 2.4GB (consistent with cryptocurrency miner using RAM for mining)

attack_flow_hypothesis.stage_1_exploit: Attacker exploits unidentified vulnerability in NestJS API to achieve RCE
attack_flow_hypothesis.stage_2_lateral_movement: From Node.js container (user: node), attacker establishes connection to PostgreSQL database
attack_flow_hypothesis.stage_3_privilege_escalation:
Attacker uses PostgreSQL to download/execute malware binary:
  - CREATE FUNCTION with plpgsql or untrusted language
  - Execute shell commands with postgres user privileges
  - Or: SQL injection providing db command execution
attack_flow_hypothesis.stage_4_persistence:
Malware placed in /tmp with persistence mechanism:
  - Crontab entry running malware at intervals
  - Init script or systemd service
  - Watchdog process that respawns on kill
attack_flow_hypothesis.stage_5_obfuscation: Binary named /tmp/mysql to blend with PostgreSQL context
attack_flow_hypothesis.stage_6_execution: Cryptocurrency miner starts, begins consuming CPU for crypto mining

why_postgres_user.reason_1: PostgreSQL can execute OS commands via contrib modules (pg_execute_sql, etc.)
why_postgres_user.reason_2: User 999 is the postgres service user inside container - hardcoded in PostgreSQL Dockerfile
why_postgres_user.reason_3: Lateral movement from Node.js → Database → OS command execution pathway
why_postgres_user.reason_4: Postgres user has /tmp write access within container

respawn_mechanism_analysis.observation: Process respawned as PID 67839 within 2 seconds after kill -9
possible_causes: Cron job running every 1-5 seconds checking and respawning /tmp/mysql
PostgreSQL function/trigger that spawns process on schedule
Init script or systemd timer executing malware
Watchdog daemon (inotify-based) monitoring /tmp/mysql and respawning if deleted
respawn_mechanism_analysis.container_context: Standard PostgreSQL 15 Docker image doesn't have obvious cron/init mechanisms, suggesting attacker added custom persistence

detection_evasion_techniques_used.explanation: Named /tmp/mysql to appear as database-related process, not obviously malicious
detection_evasion_techniques_used.detection_bypass: Simple ps aux would show suspicious process, but name helps obfuscate intent

detection_evasion_techniques_used.explanation: Placed in /tmp (temporary filesystem) not persistent volumes - leaves no trace after restart
detection_evasion_techniques_used.detection_bypass: No forensic evidence persists after container restart

detection_evasion_techniques_used.explanation: Files owned by postgres:postgres with 700 permissions - only owner can read
detection_evasion_techniques_used.detection_bypass: Admin viewing files as root could read, but non-root users could not

detection_evasion_techniques_used.explanation: Malware runs in background, doesn't interact with tty/stdout
detection_evasion_techniques_used.detection_bypass: Silent execution, CPU usage is the only obvious indicator

immediate_actions_0_to_24_hours: Patch all CRITICAL CVEs in dependencies
Rebuild container image with non-root user
Add firewall rules to restrict database port access
Review API access logs for exploitation attempts
short_term_0_to_1_week: Complete input validation on all endpoints
Implement rate limiting on authentication endpoints
Implement secrets management (Docker Secrets or external vault)
Set up monitoring/alerting for resource exhaustion
medium_term_1_to_4_weeks: Database query logging and audit trails

conclusion.security_posture: HIGH RISK - Multiple critical vulnerabilities present that could enable RCE similar to the cryptocurrency miner incident
primary_concerns: Unpatched dependencies with command injection vulnerabilities
Container running with insufficient security restrictions
Database credentials exposed and port accessible from internet
Lack of input validation on API endpoints
No apparent monitoring or rate limiting on sensitive endpoints
conclusion.attack_surface: Very High - Exposed database port, unpatched dependencies, and weak authentication on PIN endpoints create multiple exploitation paths
conclusion.likelihood_of_similar_attack: HIGH - Same vulnerabilities that allowed cryptocurrency miner installation remain present
conclusion.recommendation: Deploy all HIGH and CRITICAL fixes immediately. The application is vulnerable to the same attack vectors that led to the cryptocurrency miner incident.
